metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `_WORDS_CACHE` and `_WARNED_MISSING` globals are partitioned between the v1 (`compute_alias`) and v2 (`compute_genesis_alias`) loaders in `_alias.py` — e.g. separate module-level variables `_WORDS_CACHE_V2`/`_WARNED_MISSING_V2`, or split into a second module.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `compute_genesis_alias` will live in `src/rebar/_alias.py` alongside the existing `compute_alias`, but both functions would share the same module-level `_WORDS_CACHE` and `_WARNED_MISSING` globals. The current globals are typed as a single `tuple[list[str], list[str]] | None` cache for ONE wordlist file. A second function loading a different file (`ticket-wordlist-v2.txt`) from the same module would either silently read the wrong cached words (if v1 loaded first) or clobber v1's cache (if v2 loads first), breaking the legacy backfill path.
findings.location: Scope/Design — 'New generator `compute_genesis_alias` in `src/rebar/_alias.py`'; `src/rebar/_alias.py` lines 19-20 (`_WORDS_CACHE`, `_WARNED_MISSING`)
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce separate `_WORDS_CACHE_V2: tuple[list[str], list[str]] | None = None` and `_WARNED_MISSING_V2: bool = False` module-level globals for the v2 loader, keeping v1 globals untouched.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate in the plan whether `compute_genesis_alias` in `_alias.py` uses separate module-level cache/warn globals (e.g., `_WORDS_CACHE_V2`, `_WARNED_MISSING_V2`) distinct from the v1 `_WORDS_CACHE`/`_WARNED_MISSING`, to prevent loader state collision.
findings.finding: `compute_genesis_alias` is placed in the existing `src/rebar/_alias.py`, which already has module-level globals `_WORDS_CACHE` and `_WARNED_MISSING` tightly coupled to the v1 wordlist loader. The plan does not enumerate whether separate cache/warn state globals will be added for the v2 wordlist, creating a risk that the two loaders silently share or collide on module-level state.
findings.location: Scope/Design — `src/rebar/_alias.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Scope/Design section: 'A second pair of module-level globals `_V2_WORDS_CACHE` and `_V2_WARNED_MISSING` will be added alongside a `_wordlist_path_v2()` and `_load_v2()` to keep the two loaders fully independent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name a separate module-level cache variable for the v2 wordlist loader (e.g., `_V2_WORDS_CACHE`) in the plan, to prevent accidental reuse or overwrite of the existing `_WORDS_CACHE` used by `compute_alias`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a second wordlist loader (compute_genesis_alias) in src/rebar/_alias.py alongside the existing loader, but the existing module-level cache (_WORDS_CACHE) is typed and populated as a 2-tuple (adjs, nouns). A second loader for (adjs, animals) must use a separate cache variable; the plan does not name or specify this cache, leaving the implementation to guess at the isolation boundary.
findings.location: Scope / Design — 'New generator compute_genesis_alias in src/rebar/_alias.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_V2_WORDS_CACHE: tuple[list[str], list[str]] | None = None` and `_V2_WARNED_MISSING: bool = False` as dedicated module-level state for the new loader.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism that enforces adjective distinctness (e.g., if `adj1_idx == adj2_idx`, increment `adj2_idx` by 1 mod `len(adjs)`, or use a different hex window) so AC-1's guarantee is implementable and verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the two adjectives will be 'guaranteed distinct' but provides no mechanism description. With a simple hex-nibble modulo approach (as used in the legacy generator), any ticket ID where int(hex[0:4],16) % len(adjs) == int(hex[4:8],16) % len(adjs) produces two identical adjectives, silently violating the guarantee stated in AC-1.
findings.location: Scope / Design — 'The two adjectives are guaranteed distinct; deterministic off the hex id' / AC-1
findings.suggested_fix: When deriving adj2, if `int(hex_id[4:8], 16) % len(adjs) == adj1_idx`, use `(adj1_idx + 1) % len(adjs)` as adj2. Document this rule in the plan so tests can pin it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md/design-doc section capturing the dual-strategy alias decision: why `compute_genesis_alias` was introduced as a separate function, that `compute_alias` is frozen for legacy backfill only, and that the CREATE event is the lock-in point for format.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a dual-strategy alias architecture (legacy `compute_alias` + new `compute_genesis_alias`) and a new behavioral contract for ticket aliases, but includes no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry capturing why the split exists, when each strategy is used, and what the migration path looks like.
findings.location: Scope/Design section; `src/rebar/_alias.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/decisions/alias-v2-adjective-adjective-animal.md` (or equivalent) with: (1) context — legacy format, (2) decision — new tickets use compute_genesis_alias, legacy read-time backfill unchanged, (3) consequences — two wordlists/generators must be kept in sync independently. Reference it from CLAUDE.md if one exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `_processors.py`'s `process_create` backfill needs to be updated to call `compute_genesis_alias` (or a format-detecting dispatcher) for new-format tickets, or explicitly document why the stored-alias-always-present guarantee makes this unnecessary.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `reducer/_processors.py`'s `process_create` backfill path is safe for new tickets because 'New tickets persist the alias onto the CREATE event, locking in the format.' However, the actual backfill at `_processors.py:82-84` calls `compute_alias` (v1, adj-noun-noun) unconditionally when `data.alias` is absent. If a new ticket's CREATE event ever arrives without a stored alias (import, manual replay, or the v2 wordlist being missing at write-time causing the fallback hex alias to be stored), `process_create` will backfill with the v1 algorithm and produce an adj-noun-noun alias for a ticket that was intended to have an adj-adj-animal alias. The plan does not address updating this backfill path.
findings.location: Scope/Design — 'Wired into the create path only'; `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` lines 78-84

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the plan scoping whether delete.py should prefer the stored alias from the CREATE event (via resolve_ticket_id or direct file read) over compute_alias for its grep search term, to avoid missing v2-format alias references.
findings.finding: delete.py:68 calls compute_alias(deleted_id) to add the alias as an extra grep search term when scanning for references to the deleted ticket. After this change, new tickets have their adj-adj-animal alias stored on the CREATE event, but compute_alias returns the legacy adj-noun-noun string. The grep pattern therefore misses any LINK/SNAPSHOT files that stored a reference using the actual v2 alias string.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/delete.py:68 / Scope & Design — 'Wired into the create path only'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document the complete license chain for the gfycat word-list data embedded in `a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` (confirm the MIT license covers the *data* not just the npm wrapper code) and cite the specific upstream commit/release and license file URL in `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` npm package (and the underlying gfycat wordlists) carry an MIT license, but does not cite the actual license text or file path, and the gfycat word-list provenance is known to be informal — no dedicated license was ever published by gfycat for these lists. The plan's AC4 ('Wordlist sourced from OSS with license vendored') and the Scope/Design ('MIT') both rely on this unverified assertion.
findings.location: Scope/Design — 'vendored OSS wordlist (gfycat lists from a-type/adjective-adjective-animal, MIT)' and AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Check the `a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` GitHub repo for a `LICENSE` file and confirm its text covers the word-list data. Also check whether the gfycat source lists (often referenced as `@jsmith/gfycat-style-urls` or similar) carry their own license. If the data license is only on the npm wrapper, note that explicitly. Commit both the license text and a one-paragraph provenance note (upstream URL + commit SHA + what the license covers) into `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact section-delimiter format of the `a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` upstream wordlist (e.g., verify the marker is literally `# ANIMALS`) before finalising the parser spec, and add an AC or test that asserts the parsed animal list is non-empty.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the upstream `a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` wordlist uses a `# ANIMALS` section marker to separate adjectives from animals, but this specific file-format claim is unverified anywhere in the codebase or plan. If the actual upstream file uses a different delimiter or format, the new `_load()`-analog for v2 would silently misclassify words (e.g., placing animal words into the adjectives list), producing malformed aliases without any parse error.
findings.location: Scope / Design — '`# ANIMALS` marker' and AC1 'adjective-adjective-animal alias whose two adjectives differ'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to AC5: 'A sanity assertion confirms the loaded animal list is non-empty (guards against marker-name drift during vendoring).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC#5 as an observable outcome, e.g. 'The test suite includes at least one parametrized test asserting the new 3-word format and distinct adjectives, one test asserting determinism (same id → same alias across calls), and one test asserting the missing-wordlist fallback emits a WARN and returns a hex alias; all pass in CI.'
findings.finding: AC#5 ('Tests cover the new format, path independence, and the missing-wordlist fallback') is effort-framed — it describes what to write, not an observable, measurable outcome — making it impossible to determine when it is satisfied without a definition of coverage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullet 5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test case for the `too-short (<12 hex) id → None` path in `compute_genesis_alias` to cover the invalid-input boundary specified in AC3.
findings.finding: AC3 explicitly specifies that a too-short (<12 hex) ticket id must return None, but AC5's stated test scope ('new format, path independence, and the missing-wordlist fallback') does not include this invalid-input boundary case, leaving a specified failure path untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In the test file for `_alias.py`, add a parametrized test: ids of length 0, 1, 11 each assert `compute_genesis_alias(id) is None`, and length 12 asserts a non-None 3-word alias.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (Scope/Design or AC3) whether `_compute_alias` in `composer.py` retains its `or ticket_id.replace('-', '')[:8]` hex-fallback guard after delegating to `compute_genesis_alias`, or whether `compute_genesis_alias` fully absorbs that fallback.
findings.finding: The plan states `_compute_alias` in `composer.py` 'now calls `compute_genesis_alias`', but does not specify whether the existing hex-fallback guard (`or ticket_id.replace('-', '')[:8]`) in `_compute_alias` is preserved, delegated into `compute_genesis_alias`, or dropped — leaving the edit target's post-change behavior ambiguous.
findings.location: Scope/Design — `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` `_compute_alias`; AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step to create `docs/licenses/` and confirm the license file path in a test or CI check, given that the directory does not yet exist in the repo.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the license will be vendored to `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt`, but the `docs/licenses/` directory does not exist in the repository. The plan does not mention that this directory needs to be created, nor does any AC verify the license file is actually present and accessible.
findings.location: Scope/Design — 'License vendored to `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt`'; AC bullet 'Wordlist sourced from OSS with license vendored'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (and a corresponding integration/end-to-end test) that creates a ticket via the normal create path and asserts the resulting persisted alias matches the adjective-adjective-animal format, confirming the composer.py wiring is exercised.
findings.finding: The cutover wiring in composer.py (_compute_alias now calls compute_genesis_alias) has no acceptance criterion or described test that exercises the full ticket-creation code path end-to-end, so a green unit-test suite on the generator alone would not prove that newly created tickets actually carry the new alias format in production.
findings.location: Scope/Design — 'Wired into the create path only: src/rebar/_commands/composer.py _compute_alias now calls compute_genesis_alias'; AC#1
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'An integration test creates a ticket through the standard create flow and asserts the persisted alias on the CREATE event matches ^[a-z]+-[a-z]+-[a-z]+$ with three distinct words.' Map this to a test in the integration test suite (e.g., tests/integration/test_create_alias.py) that calls the create command and inspects the stored event.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope/Design section an explicit description of `compute_genesis_alias` edge-case behavior: what it returns for ids shorter than 12 hex chars (None) and what happens when the bundled wordlist is missing (hex alias fallback + one-shot WARN log), so AC3 has corresponding described design rather than being an orphan criterion.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies two behaviors — (a) too-short (<12 hex) id returns None and (b) missing wordlist falls back to a hex alias with a one-shot WARN — that have no corresponding description in the Scope/Design section. These are non-trivial implementation decisions (sentinel return value, fallback strategy, logging level) with no design rationale, leaving them as orphan acceptance criteria.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 vs. Scope / Design section
findings.suggested_fix: Under Scope/Design, add a bullet: 'compute_genesis_alias returns None for ids shorter than 12 hex chars. If the bundled wordlist file is absent at import time, the function logs a one-shot WARN and returns a plain hex alias, ensuring the create path never hard-fails due to a missing resource.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'path independence' in AC5 (or the Scope/Design section) — e.g., 'the wordlist is loaded correctly regardless of the process working directory' — so the test requirement is unambiguous and verifiable.
findings.finding: AC5 references 'path independence' as a test requirement, but this term is undefined anywhere in the plan — it is unclear whether it means filesystem path independence (the wordlist can be loaded from different working directories), import-path independence, or something else.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale to the Scope/Design section explaining why a separate `compute_genesis_alias` + new wordlist is chosen over a versioned/extended `compute_alias` (e.g., 'A new independent function preserves the legacy function as a pure constant, eliminating any risk of wordlist or logic drift that would silently change existing aliases').
findings.finding: The plan states the design choice (separate function + new wordlist, legacy unchanged) but does not give a positive rationale for WHY a new function rather than extending the existing compute_alias with a format-version flag. The absence of an affirmative 'why this fits' explanation leaves implementers without guidance if the approach is questioned during review.
findings.location: Scope / Design section — no positive rationale paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art note to the plan (or a linked ADR) explaining why a vendored wordlist is preferred over an existing library (e.g. `coolname`, `petname`) — specifically whether the 'stdlib-only leaf' constraint, install-size, or offline-operation requirement rules out a library dependency.
findings.finding: The plan does not show that established Python libraries for deterministic human-readable ID generation (e.g. `coolname`, `petname`, `haikunator`) were evaluated before committing to a vendored raw-wordlist approach. These libraries offer adj-adj-animal or adj-animal styles, support seeded/deterministic generation, carry independently verified MIT/Apache licenses, and would eliminate the wordlist-vendoring and license-chain problem entirely.
findings.location: Scope/Design — 'backed by a new bundled wordlist `src/rebar/_engine/resources/ticket-wordlist-v2.txt`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan to note that `docs/licenses/` is a new directory that will be created as part of vendoring the license file.
findings.finding: The plan vendors a license file to `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt`, but the `docs/licenses/` directory does not exist in the repository — only `docs/adr/`, `docs/archive/`, `docs/calibration/`, `docs/design/`, and `docs/experiments/` subdirectories exist. The plan does not acknowledge that a new directory must be created.
findings.location: Scope/Design — 'License vendored to `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt`'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan line to: 'License vendored to `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt` (new `docs/licenses/` directory created).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit existing docs/README/changelog for references to the `adjective-noun-noun` alias format and update them (or add a note) to reflect that new tickets use `adjective-adjective-animal`.
findings.finding: Existing user-facing or developer documentation that describes the ticket alias format as `adjective-noun-noun` will be stale after this change; the plan does not mention auditing or updating such references.
findings.location: Scope/Design section — 'Existing tickets are untouched and keep resolving by their legacy aliases'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `compute_genesis_alias` needs to be a separately-named top-level export or whether the logic can instead be inlined into `_compute_alias` in `composer.py` (one call-site, no other consumers), and if kept separate, confirm the v2 loader globals won't share or collide with the existing v1 `_WORDS_CACHE`/`_WARNED_MISSING` state.
findings.finding: `compute_genesis_alias` has exactly one planned call-site (composer.py `_compute_alias`), so introducing it as a named top-level function fails the Rule-of-Three heuristic for new abstractions; the v2 loading infrastructure it requires (new cache global, new path resolver, new warned-flag) also replicates the existing `_load`/`_WORDS_CACHE`/`_WARNED_MISSING`/`_wordlist_path` pattern already in `_alias.py`, amounting to NIH of loader infrastructure from the same module.
findings.location: Scope/Design — `compute_genesis_alias` in `src/rebar/_alias.py`; `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` `_compute_alias`
findings.suggested_fix: Inline the v2 wordlist selection directly into `composer.py`'s `_compute_alias` (the sole call-site), keeping the shared loader infrastructure in `_alias.py` generic (pass a wordlist path parameter to `_load`) rather than adding a parallel stack. If `compute_genesis_alias` must be importable for testing, extract it only after a second call-site exists.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
